script:
Let's talk about what this program is going to do for you.

This lesson introduces the full 16-module HVAC training program. You will learn the weekly schedule, what credentials you earn (EPA 608 Universal, OSHA 10, CPR/AED), how the classroom and lab time is structured, and what employers expect from graduates. The HVAC industry has a severe technician shortage — the Bureau of Labor Statistics projects 13% job growth through 2031, much faster than average. Starting wages for certified technicians range from $16-22/hr, with experienced techs earning $25-40/hr. This program prepares you for immediate employment as an apprentice technician.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

EPA 608: Federal certification required by the Clean Air Act to purchase or handle any refrigerant. Without it, you cannot legally work on AC or refrigeration systems.

OSHA 10: 10-hour construction safety certification delivered through CareerSafe. DOL wallet card issued upon completion. Required by most HVAC employers.

ETPL: Eligible Training Provider List — state-approved programs that qualify for WIOA workforce funding.

Here's why this matters on the job: Understanding the career pathway from day one helps you set realistic expectations. Employers want technicians who understand the licensing process and are committed to building hours toward journeyman status.

Watch out for these: Do not skip the documentation requirements — missing paperwork delays your funding. And — Register at indianacareerconnect.com immediately if you have not already

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Let's talk about what this program is going to do for you.

WIOA (Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act) is the primary funding source for most students in this program. This lesson explains exactly what WIOA covers: tuition, tools, transportation, childcare, work clothing, and certification exam fees. You will learn the eligibility requirements, how to work with your WorkOne case manager, and what documentation you need. Other funding options include the Workforce Ready Grant (WRG) for Indiana residents, Justice Reinvestment Initiative (JRI) for justice-involved individuals, and self-pay with flexible payment plans.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

WIOA: Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act — federal funding for job training administered through local WorkOne offices.

WRG: Workforce Ready Grant — Indiana state funding for high-demand certifications, no income requirements.

ITA: Individual Training Account — the WIOA funding mechanism that pays your tuition directly to the training provider.

Here's why this matters on the job: Understanding your funding keeps you enrolled. Students who lose funding usually lose it because they missed documentation deadlines or attendance requirements — not because they were ineligible.

Watch out for these: Keep copies of every document you submit. And — Respond to your case manager within 24 hours — they control your funding

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Let's talk about what this program is going to do for you.

The HVAC industry offers four career levels: Apprentice (0-2 years, $16-22/hr), Journeyman (2-5 years, $22-32/hr), Master Technician (5-10 years, $32-45/hr), and Contractor/Business Owner ($50-100K+ annually). This lesson maps each level to the certifications, hours, and skills required. You will also learn about specialization paths: residential service, commercial HVAC, refrigeration, building automation, and energy management. The skilled trades are experiencing a generational workforce gap — millions of technicians are retiring and not enough new workers are entering the field.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Journeyman: Licensed technician who has completed required OJT hours (typically 2,000+) and passed the state exam. Can work independently.

Master Technician: Experienced technician with advanced certifications (NATE, manufacturer-specific) who can diagnose complex system problems.

NATE: North American Technician Excellence — the leading third-party certification for HVAC technicians, recognized by all major manufacturers.

Here's why this matters on the job: Knowing the career ladder helps you negotiate your first job. An employer offering $16/hr with a clear path to journeyman is often better than $18/hr with no advancement plan.

Watch out for these: Track your OJT hours from day one — you will need documentation for licensure. And — Ask potential employers about their apprenticeship structure during interviews

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Let's talk about what this program is going to do for you.

This quiz covers program structure, funding options, attendance requirements, documentation needs, and career pathways. You must score 70% or higher to proceed. The quiz tests whether you understand the practical requirements of staying enrolled and funded — not technical HVAC knowledge yet.

Here's why this matters on the job: Employers value technicians who understand the business side of the trade, not just the technical side.

Watch out for these: Review the attendance and documentation requirements before taking this quiz. And — Know the difference between WIOA, WRG, and JRI funding

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Before you touch a single tool, you need to understand what HVAC systems actually do.

Every HVAC system has the same basic job: move heat from where you do not want it to where you do. In cooling mode, the system moves heat from inside the building to outside. In heating mode, it moves heat from a fuel source (gas, electricity, or outdoor air) into the building. This lesson covers the complete system: outdoor condenser unit, indoor air handler or furnace, evaporator coil, refrigerant lines (suction and liquid), ductwork (supply and return), thermostat, and electrical connections. You will learn how these components connect and why each one is necessary. The split-system air conditioner is the most common residential configuration — you will service this system more than any other in your career.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Split System: An HVAC system with components split between outdoor (condenser) and indoor (air handler/furnace) units, connected by refrigerant lines.

Condenser: The outdoor unit containing the compressor, condenser coil, and fan motor. Rejects heat to the outdoor air.

Evaporator: The indoor coil where cold refrigerant absorbs heat from room air. Located above the furnace or inside the air handler.

Here's why this matters on the job: On every service call, you must quickly identify all system components and understand how they connect. A technician who cannot trace the refrigerant circuit or airflow path cannot diagnose problems.

Watch out for these: The suction line is ALWAYS the larger of the two refrigerant lines — it carries low-pressure vapor. And — Never confuse the supply duct (conditioned air out) with the return duct (room air back)

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Before you touch a single tool, you need to understand what HVAC systems actually do.

This lesson covers every tool in your HVAC toolkit and how to use each one correctly. The digital multimeter measures voltage, resistance, continuity, and capacitance — you will use it on every single service call. The clamp-on ammeter measures current without disconnecting wires. The manifold gauge set connects to the system service ports to measure suction and discharge pressure — the blue gauge reads low-side pressure, the red gauge reads high-side pressure, and the yellow center hose connects to the refrigerant cylinder or vacuum pump. You will also learn to use thermometers (pipe clamp and digital), leak detectors (electronic and UV dye), vacuum pumps, recovery machines, refrigerant scales, tube cutters, flaring tools, swaging tools, and hand tools specific to HVAC work.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Multimeter: Measures voltage (V), resistance (Ω), continuity, and capacitance (µF). The most-used diagnostic tool in HVAC.

Manifold Gauge Set: Two pressure gauges (blue=low side, red=high side) with valves and hoses for measuring system pressures and charging refrigerant.

Clamp Meter: Measures amperage by clamping around a wire without disconnecting it. Used to check motor amp draw against nameplate ratings.

Here's why this matters on the job: A technician without proper tools cannot diagnose anything. Employers expect you to own a basic toolkit. Many WIOA programs cover tool costs — take advantage of this.

Watch out for these: Always zero your multimeter before measuring resistance. And — Never connect the high-side gauge hose to the low-side port — check the line sizes

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Before you touch a single tool, you need to understand what HVAC systems actually do.

HVAC work involves serious hazards: high voltage (240V+), refrigerant exposure (frostbite, asphyxiation in confined spaces), working at heights (rooftops, ladders), hot surfaces (brazing, furnace heat exchangers), and rotating machinery (fan blades, blower motors). This lesson covers required PPE: safety glasses (always), work gloves (when handling sheet metal or hot surfaces), steel-toe boots, hearing protection (near compressors), and respiratory protection (when working with chemicals or in dusty environments). You will learn lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures — the process of disconnecting and verifying zero energy before working on equipment. LOTO is not optional. Technicians die every year from equipment that was not properly de-energized.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

LOTO: Lockout/Tagout — the procedure of disconnecting power, locking the disconnect, and verifying 0 volts before working on equipment.

PPE: Personal Protective Equipment — safety glasses, gloves, boots, hearing protection, and respiratory protection as required by the task.

Arc Flash: An electrical explosion caused by a short circuit through air. Can cause severe burns and death. Proper PPE and de-energization prevent arc flash incidents.

Here's why this matters on the job: Safety violations get you fired immediately. No employer will tolerate a technician who skips LOTO or does not wear PPE. More importantly, safety keeps you alive.

Watch out for these: ALWAYS verify 0 volts with YOUR OWN multimeter after pulling the disconnect — never trust someone else. And — Refrigerant is heavier than air — it sinks to the floor in enclosed spaces and displaces oxygen

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Before you touch a single tool, you need to understand what HVAC systems actually do.

In this lab, you will physically identify every major component on a residential split-system air conditioner. You will locate the compressor, condenser coil, fan motor, capacitor, contactor, and service valves on the outdoor unit. On the indoor unit, you will identify the evaporator coil, blower motor, air filter, control board, and thermostat connections. You will trace the refrigerant circuit from the compressor discharge through the condenser coil, liquid line, metering device, evaporator coil, and suction line back to the compressor. You will also trace the airflow path from the return grille through the filter, across the evaporator coil, through the supply ductwork, and out the supply registers.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Discharge Line: The hot gas line from the compressor outlet to the condenser coil inlet. Hot to the touch during operation.

Liquid Line: The smaller copper line carrying warm high-pressure liquid from the condenser to the metering device.

Suction Line: The larger insulated copper line carrying cold low-pressure vapor from the evaporator back to the compressor.

Here's why this matters on the job: Component identification is the foundation of every service call. If you cannot find the capacitor in 30 seconds, you cannot diagnose a no-cooling call efficiently.

Watch out for these: The capacitor stores electrical charge even when power is off — always discharge it before handling. And — The contactor may be stuck closed (welded contacts) — check before assuming the system is off

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Before you touch a single tool, you need to understand what HVAC systems actually do.

This quiz tests your understanding of HVAC system components, tool identification and usage, safety procedures, and PPE requirements. You must identify components by description, match tools to their diagnostic purpose, and demonstrate knowledge of LOTO procedures.

Here's why this matters on the job: Employers test new hires on basic component identification and safety knowledge before allowing them on job sites.

Watch out for these: Know the difference between the suction line and liquid line. And — Know which PPE is required for each type of task

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Electricity runs everything in an HVAC system. If you don't understand it, you can't diagnose anything.

Electricity is the language of HVAC systems. Every component — compressor, fan motor, blower, control board, thermostat — runs on electricity. This lesson teaches the three fundamental electrical properties: Voltage (V) is electrical pressure — the force that pushes electrons through a circuit. Current (I, measured in Amps) is the flow of electrons — how much electricity is moving. Resistance (R, measured in Ohms) is opposition to current flow. Ohm's Law ties them together: V = I × R. If you know any two values, you can calculate the third. Watt's Law adds power: P = V × I. A 240V compressor drawing 15 amps uses 3,600 watts (3.6 kW). You will use these calculations on every service call to determine if motors are drawing correct amperage, if circuits are properly sized, and if components have failed.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Voltage (V): Electrical pressure measured in volts. Residential HVAC uses 24V (control), 120V (blower), and 240V (compressor, heat strips).

Amperage (I): Electrical current measured in amps. Compare measured amps to the nameplate rating to determine if a motor is working correctly.

Resistance (Ω): Opposition to current flow measured in ohms. A good wire has near-zero ohms. An open circuit has infinite ohms.

Here's why this matters on the job: When a compressor nameplate says RLA 14.0 and you measure 18.5 amps, you know something is wrong — dirty condenser coil, low voltage, or failing compressor. Without understanding amps, you cannot make this diagnosis.

Watch out for these: Always measure voltage BEFORE measuring resistance — never measure resistance on an energized circuit. And — A motor drawing MORE than RLA is overloaded. A motor drawing LESS than RLA may have a mechanical problem.

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Electricity runs everything in an HVAC system. If you don't understand it, you can't diagnose anything.

Wiring diagrams are the roadmaps of HVAC systems. There are two types: schematic diagrams (show the electrical logic — which components connect to which) and pictorial diagrams (show the physical location of components and wire routing). This lesson teaches you to read both. You will trace the 24V control circuit from the transformer through the thermostat to the contactor coil. You will trace the 240V power circuit from the disconnect through the contactor contacts to the compressor and fan motor. You will learn standard electrical symbols: switches, relays, transformers, motors, capacitors, and ground connections. Every manufacturer includes wiring diagrams on the equipment — usually on the inside of the access panel. Learning to read them is not optional.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Schematic Diagram: Shows electrical connections logically — which components are in series or parallel. Used for troubleshooting circuit logic.

Pictorial Diagram: Shows physical wire routing and component locations. Used for finding components and tracing wires on the actual equipment.

Series Circuit: Components connected end-to-end. Current flows through each one. If any component opens, the entire circuit stops. Safety switches are wired in series.

Here's why this matters on the job: On a no-cooling call, you trace the 24V circuit: transformer → thermostat → safety switches → contactor coil. If you find 24V at the thermostat but 0V at the contactor, the problem is between those two points. Without reading the diagram, you are guessing.

Watch out for these: Always check the wiring diagram on the equipment — do not assume all systems are wired the same. And — A broken wire in a series circuit stops everything downstream

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Electricity runs everything in an HVAC system. If you don't understand it, you can't diagnose anything.

In this lab, you will use a digital multimeter to measure AC voltage (at the disconnect, contactor, and transformer), DC voltage (at the control board), resistance (across motor windings and capacitors), continuity (through switches and fuses), and capacitance (on run capacitors). You will use a clamp-on ammeter to measure motor amperage on the compressor and fan motor, comparing readings to nameplate values. You will practice safe measurement techniques: selecting the correct meter function, using the correct probe ports, and never measuring resistance on an energized circuit.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Continuity: A complete electrical path. Your meter beeps when continuity exists (near-zero ohms). No beep = open circuit (broken wire, blown fuse, open switch).

Capacitance: The ability to store electrical charge, measured in microfarads (µF). A capacitor must be within ±6% of its rated value to work properly.

Common (C) Terminal: On a compressor, the terminal connected to both the start and run windings. Used as a reference point for winding resistance measurements.

Here's why this matters on the job: Every service call requires meter readings. Measuring voltage confirms power. Measuring amps confirms motor health. Measuring capacitance confirms capacitor condition. These three measurements solve 80% of electrical problems.

Watch out for these: Set your meter to the correct function BEFORE connecting probes — wrong setting can damage the meter or shock you. And — Discharge capacitors before measuring capacitance — stored charge gives false readings and can shock you

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Electricity runs everything in an HVAC system. If you don't understand it, you can't diagnose anything.

Capacitors, contactors, and relays are the three most commonly replaced electrical components in HVAC. A run capacitor stores and releases energy to keep motors running efficiently — when it fails, the motor hums but does not start, or starts slowly and overheats. Capacitors are rated in microfarads (µF) and voltage. A dual run capacitor has three terminals: HERM (compressor), FAN (condenser fan), and C (common). A contactor is an electrically controlled switch — 24V from the thermostat energizes the coil, which magnetically pulls the contacts closed, sending 240V to the motors. Pitted or burned contacts cause voltage drop and overheating. A relay is a smaller version of a contactor, used for lower-current switching like fan relays and time-delay relays.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Run Capacitor: Provides continuous phase shift to keep a motor running efficiently. Failure causes humming, slow starting, or motor overheating.

Start Capacitor: Provides a brief high-torque boost during motor startup, then disconnects. Used on hard-start kits.

Dual Run Capacitor: Single capacitor with two sections — one for the compressor (HERM) and one for the fan motor (FAN), sharing a common terminal (C).

Here's why this matters on the job: Bad capacitors are the #1 cause of no-cooling calls in summer. A technician who can diagnose and replace a capacitor in 15 minutes is worth their weight in gold to an employer.

Watch out for these: Always replace a capacitor with the EXACT µF rating — wrong value damages the motor. And — A bulging or leaking capacitor is failed — replace it even if it still reads in range

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Electricity runs everything in an HVAC system. If you don't understand it, you can't diagnose anything.

This quiz tests Ohm's Law calculations, meter usage, wiring diagram reading, capacitor diagnosis, contactor operation, and electrical safety. You must be able to calculate voltage, current, or resistance given the other two values, identify the correct meter function for each measurement, and trace a circuit on a wiring diagram.

Here's why this matters on the job: Electrical knowledge separates a parts-changer from a real technician. Anyone can swap a capacitor — a technician understands WHY it failed and checks for the root cause.

Watch out for these: Know Ohm's Law cold: V=IR, I=V/R, R=V/I. And — Know the difference between series and parallel circuits

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Heating systems keep people alive in winter. You need to know them cold.

A gas furnace burns natural gas or propane to heat air. The sequence: thermostat calls for heat, draft inducer starts, pressure switch closes, igniter glows at 1800F+, gas valve opens, burners ignite, flame sensor proves flame, heat exchanger warms, blower starts after delay, warm air flows through ducts. If any step fails, the control board shuts down. The heat exchanger separates combustion gases from breathable air. A crack allows carbon monoxide into the living space.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Heat Exchanger: Metal chambers transferring combustion heat to room air. A crack leaks CO.

Flame Sensor: Thin metal rod proving flame by conducting microamps. Dirty flame sensor is the number one no-heat call.

Here's why this matters on the job: Gas furnace calls are winter bread and butter. Diagnosing no-heat in 15 minutes keeps you busy all season.

Watch out for these: Read control board LED fault codes FIRST. And — Dirty flame sensor is the most common no-heat cause

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Heating systems keep people alive in winter. You need to know them cold.

Electric heat uses resistance elements called heat strips. Nichrome wire generates heat when current flows through it. Strips are staged through a sequencer relay to prevent inrush current. A 15kW system might have three 5kW stages. Electric heat is 100 percent efficient but expensive. Heat strips serve as auxiliary and emergency heat in heat pump systems.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Heat Strip: Nichrome wire resistance element converting electricity to heat.

Sequencer: Time-delay relay staging heat strips on one at a time.

Here's why this matters on the job: High winter electric bills often mean heat strips running constantly because the heat pump has a problem.

Watch out for these: Measure amp draw on each element. And — Emergency heat costs 2-3x more than heat pump operation

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Heating systems keep people alive in winter. You need to know them cold.

A heat pump is an AC that runs in reverse. A reversing valve switches refrigerant flow direction. In cooling mode the outdoor coil rejects heat. In heating mode the outdoor coil absorbs heat from outdoor air. The O wire energizes the reversing valve in cooling mode on Carrier and Honeywell systems. The B wire energizes it in heating mode on Rheem and Ruud systems. In heating mode the outdoor coil ices up and the defrost control periodically switches to cooling mode to melt ice.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Reversing Valve: A 4-way valve changing refrigerant flow direction between heating and cooling.

Defrost Cycle: Temporarily switches to cooling to melt outdoor coil ice.

Here's why this matters on the job: Heat pumps are the fastest-growing residential HVAC segment.

Watch out for these: Know O vs B convention before diagnosing. And — Stuck reversing valve causes heating in cooling mode or vice versa

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Heating systems keep people alive in winter. You need to know them cold.

Combustion analysis verifies safe efficient furnace operation. A combustion analyzer measures CO, O2, CO2, and stack temperature in flue gases. Gas pressure is measured with a manometer. Natural gas should be 3.5 inches water column at the manifold. Incorrect pressure causes incomplete combustion and carbon monoxide production.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Combustion Analyzer: Measures CO, O2, CO2, stack temp in flue gases.

Manifold Pressure: Gas pressure at burners. Natural gas 3.5 in w.c.

Here's why this matters on the job: Combustion analysis is required during installation and annual maintenance.

Watch out for these: Any CO above 9 ppm in living space requires investigation. And — Never adjust gas pressure without a manometer

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Heating systems keep people alive in winter. You need to know them cold.

Complete furnace inspection lab covering visual heat exchanger inspection, flame sensor cleaning and microamp measurement, igniter resistance check, gas pressure measurement, burner flame inspection, blower amp draw, filter replacement, safety switch testing, control board fault codes, and thermostat verification. Document findings on a professional service report.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Temperature Rise: Supply minus return air temp across furnace. Must be within nameplate range.

Here's why this matters on the job: Furnace maintenance is major revenue for HVAC companies.

Watch out for these: Check temperature rise against nameplate range. And — Flame sensor below 1 microamp needs cleaning

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

Heating systems keep people alive in winter. You need to know them cold.

Quiz covering gas furnace operation sequence, heat exchanger safety, electric heat, heat pump operation, combustion analysis, and inspection procedures.

Here's why this matters on the job: Heating knowledge is tested on the journeyman exam.

Watch out for these: Know the complete furnace ignition sequence. And — Know what a cracked heat exchanger means

Take a minute before you move on. What's the one thing from this lesson you need to remember on a service call?

The refrigeration cycle is the foundation of everything you'll do in this trade.

The refrigeration cycle has four stages. Compression: the compressor takes low-pressure vapor and compresses it into high-pressure high-temperature gas. Condensation: hot gas flows through the condenser coil where outdoor air removes heat and refrigerant condenses into warm liquid. Expansion: the metering device creates a pressure drop converting warm liquid into cold low-pressure mixture. Evaporation: cold refrigerant flows through the evaporator coil absorbing heat from indoor air and evaporating into vapor. HVAC systems move heat, they do not create cold.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Superheat: Temperature of vapor above its boiling point at the current pressure. Measured at suction line.

Subcooling: Temperature of liquid below its condensing point at the current pressure. Measured at liquid line.

TXV: Thermostatic Expansion Valve adjusting refrigerant flow based on evaporator superheat.

Here's why this matters on the job: Every cooling diagnosis starts with the refrigeration cycle. Understanding what should happen at each stage lets you identify what is actually happening.

Watch out for these: High superheat means not enough refrigerant reaching evaporator. And — Low superheat means too much liquid risking compressor damage

Before you move on — what happens to refrigerant in the evaporator? Don't look it up. Think about it.

The refrigeration cycle is the foundation of everything you'll do in this trade.

The pressure-temperature relationship is the foundation of refrigerant diagnostics. Every refrigerant has a specific saturation temperature at each pressure. For R-410A at 118 psig the saturation temp is about 40F. You use the PT chart to convert gauge readings to temperatures. Superheat equals actual suction line temp minus suction saturation temp. Subcooling equals liquid saturation temp minus actual liquid line temp. These two calculations tell you the condition of the charge.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

PT Chart: Shows saturation temperature for each refrigerant at every pressure.

R-410A: Standard residential AC refrigerant operating at higher pressures than R-22.

Here's why this matters on the job: You will calculate superheat and subcooling on every cooling diagnostic call.

Watch out for these: Use the PT chart for the CORRECT refrigerant. And — Superheat measured at suction line, subcooling at liquid line

Before you move on — what happens to refrigerant in the evaporator? Don't look it up. Think about it.

The refrigeration cycle is the foundation of everything you'll do in this trade.

The compressor compresses low-pressure vapor into high-pressure gas. Residential systems use scroll compressors (two spiral scrolls, one orbiting) and reciprocating compressors (piston and cylinder). The motor has C, S, and R terminals. Diagnose by measuring winding resistance: C-S highest, C-R lower, S-R equals C-S plus C-R. Open winding means burned out. Grounded winding (low resistance to shell) means insulation breakdown and replacement.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Scroll Compressor: Two interlocking spirals compress refrigerant. Quieter and more efficient.

C-S-R Terminals: Common, Start, Run terminals for winding resistance diagnosis.

Here's why this matters on the job: Before condemning a compressor always check the capacitor and contactor first. Those are much cheaper.

Watch out for these: Humming compressor usually means bad capacitor not bad compressor. And — Always check capacitor and contactor BEFORE condemning compressor

Before you move on — what happens to refrigerant in the evaporator? Don't look it up. Think about it.

The refrigeration cycle is the foundation of everything you'll do in this trade.

The metering device controls refrigerant flow into the evaporator. TXV adjusts flow based on superheat using a sensing bulb on the suction line. Piston is a fixed orifice providing constant restriction. TXV systems are charged by subcooling target 10-15F. Piston systems are charged by superheat using the manufacturer charging chart. Using the wrong method gives incorrect results.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

TXV Sensing Bulb: Temperature sensor clamped to suction line controlling TXV opening.

Fixed Orifice: Piston with precisely sized hole providing constant restriction.

Here's why this matters on the job: Knowing which metering device determines your charging method. Wrong method gives wrong results.

Watch out for these: Check data plate for TXV or piston. And — Restricted TXV mimics low charge symptoms

Before you move on — what happens to refrigerant in the evaporator? Don't look it up. Think about it.

The refrigeration cycle is the foundation of everything you'll do in this trade.

Lab: connect manifold gauges to a live system and calculate superheat and subcooling. Connect blue hose to suction port, red to liquid port. Read pressures, convert to saturation temps using PT chart, measure actual line temps, calculate superheat and subcooling. Practice on systems with correct charge, undercharge, and overcharge to see how numbers change.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Target Superheat: Manufacturer-specified superheat at current conditions.

Target Subcooling: Manufacturer-specified subcooling, typically 10-15F for TXV systems.

Here's why this matters on the job: This is the single most important diagnostic skill in HVAC.

Watch out for these: Verify airflow BEFORE connecting gauges. And — Let system run 15+ minutes before readings

Before you move on — what happens to refrigerant in the evaporator? Don't look it up. Think about it.

The refrigeration cycle is the foundation of everything you'll do in this trade.

Quiz testing refrigeration cycle stages, PT chart usage, superheat and subcooling calculations, compressor diagnosis, metering device operation, and gauge reading interpretation.

Here's why this matters on the job: Refrigeration knowledge is core to EPA 608 and every job interview.

Watch out for these: Calculate superheat and subcooling from given readings. And — Know what high/low values indicate

Before you move on — what happens to refrigerant in the evaporator? Don't look it up. Think about it.

EPA 608 is not optional. You cannot legally touch refrigerant without it. Let's make sure you pass.

CFCs and HCFCs contain chlorine that destroys the ozone layer. One chlorine atom can destroy 100,000 ozone molecules. The Montreal Protocol phased out CFC production (R-12 banned 1996) and is phasing out HCFCs (R-22 production ended 2020). HFCs like R-410A have zero ozone depletion potential but high global warming potential. The Kigali Amendment phases down HFCs. Understanding this history explains why refrigerant handling regulations exist.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

CFC: Chlorofluorocarbon. Highest ozone depletion. R-12 is a CFC. Banned.

HCFC: Hydrochlorofluorocarbon. Lower ozone depletion. R-22 is an HCFC. Being phased out.

HFC: Hydrofluorocarbon. Zero ozone depletion but high GWP. R-410A is an HFC.

Here's why this matters on the job: EPA 608 exam tests this extensively. Understanding why regulations exist helps you remember the rules.

Watch out for these: Know the CFC/HCFC/HFC hierarchy. And — R-22 can no longer be manufactured but existing stocks can be used

Quick check: what is the maximum fine for knowingly venting refrigerant? If you don't know that number, go back and read it again.

EPA 608 is not optional. You cannot legally touch refrigerant without it. Let's make sure you pass.

Section 608 of the Clean Air Act makes it illegal to knowingly vent refrigerant. Fines up to $44,539 per day per violation. Technicians must be EPA 608 certified to purchase refrigerant. Recovery is required before any repair that opens the refrigerant circuit. Records must be kept for refrigerant purchases, usage, and disposal. The law applies to ALL refrigerants including HFCs.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Section 608: Clean Air Act provision regulating stationary refrigerant equipment.

Venting Prohibition: Knowingly releasing refrigerant is illegal. Fines up to $44,539 per day.

Here's why this matters on the job: Violations result in personal fines, not just company fines. You are personally liable.

Watch out for these: Venting is illegal for ALL refrigerant types. And — Fines apply to individuals not just companies

Quick check: what is the maximum fine for knowingly venting refrigerant? If you don't know that number, go back and read it again.

EPA 608 is not optional. You cannot legally touch refrigerant without it. Let's make sure you pass.

Refrigerant safety: liquid refrigerant causes frostbite on skin contact. Refrigerant vapor is heavier than air and displaces oxygen in enclosed spaces causing asphyxiation. Decomposed refrigerant (from flame contact) produces phosgene gas which is toxic. Always use PPE when handling refrigerant. Never use an open flame leak detector on systems with refrigerant. Cylinders must be stored upright, away from heat, and secured to prevent falling.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Phosgene: Toxic gas produced when refrigerant contacts an open flame. Extremely dangerous.

Asphyxiation: Suffocation from oxygen displacement. Refrigerant vapor sinks and displaces breathable air.

Here's why this matters on the job: Refrigerant safety is tested on every EPA 608 section. More importantly it keeps you alive.

Watch out for these: Refrigerant is heavier than air and sinks. And — Never use open flame leak detectors

Quick check: what is the maximum fine for knowingly venting refrigerant? If you don't know that number, go back and read it again.

EPA 608 is not optional. You cannot legally touch refrigerant without it. Let's make sure you pass.

Refrigerant classification: Group 1 (low toxicity, low flammability) includes R-410A, R-22, R-134a. Group 2 (low toxicity, mildly flammable) includes R-32. Group 3 (low toxicity, highly flammable) includes R-290 (propane). Safety group A means low toxicity, B means higher toxicity. Flammability class 1 means no flame propagation, 2L means mildly flammable, 3 means highly flammable. Cylinder colors identify refrigerants: R-410A is rose/pink, R-22 is green, R-134a is light blue.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

ASHRAE 34: Standard classifying refrigerants by toxicity and flammability.

A2L: Low toxicity, mildly flammable. Includes newer low-GWP refrigerants like R-32.

Here's why this matters on the job: Knowing refrigerant classifications prevents dangerous mixing and ensures proper handling.

Watch out for these: Never mix refrigerants. And — Cylinder color identifies the refrigerant type

Quick check: what is the maximum fine for knowingly venting refrigerant? If you don't know that number, go back and read it again.

EPA 608 is not optional. You cannot legally touch refrigerant without it. Let's make sure you pass.

Pressure-temperature fundamentals for EPA 608: every refrigerant has a unique PT relationship. At the same pressure different refrigerants have different saturation temperatures. This is why you must know which refrigerant is in the system before connecting gauges. R-410A operates at approximately 1.6 times the pressure of R-22 at the same temperature. Using R-22 gauges on an R-410A system can be dangerous because the pressures exceed the gauge range.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Saturation: The condition where liquid and vapor coexist at a given pressure and temperature.

Here's why this matters on the job: PT relationships are tested extensively on the EPA 608 exam.

Watch out for these: R-410A pressures are much higher than R-22. And — Use gauges rated for the refrigerant in the system

Recovery removes refrigerant from a system into an approved cylinder. Recycling cleans recovered refrigerant by removing oil and moisture for reuse. Reclamation processes refrigerant at a certified facility to ARI 700 purity standards. Recovery is required before any repair that opens the refrigerant circuit. Recovery equipment must be certified and tested annually. Recovery cylinders must never be filled above 80 percent capacity.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Recovery: Removing refrigerant from a system into an approved cylinder.

Recycling: Cleaning recovered refrigerant by removing oil and moisture.

Reclamation: Processing refrigerant to ARI 700 purity at a certified facility.

Here's why this matters on the job: You will recover refrigerant on almost every major repair. Proper technique prevents contamination and legal issues.

Watch out for these: Never fill recovery cylinders above 80 percent. And — Recovery equipment must be certified

Records of sales must be maintained. This regulation closed the loophole that previously allowed anyone to buy HFC refrigerants.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Sales Restriction: Only EPA 608 certified technicians can purchase any refrigerant since 2018.

Here's why this matters on the job: Without your EPA 608 card you cannot buy refrigerant. Period.

Watch out for these: Applies to ALL refrigerants including HFCs. And — Applies to ALL container sizes

Watch out for these: Know CFC/HCFC/HFC differences. And — Know venting penalties

Type I covers small appliances. Don't underestimate this section — it has tricky questions.

Type I covers factory-charged systems containing 5 lbs or less of refrigerant. This includes window AC units, PTACs, household refrigerators, freezers, dehumidifiers, water coolers, and vending machines. These systems are manufactured as sealed units and are not designed for field charging. The refrigerant circuit is typically brazed closed with no service ports.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Small Appliance: Factory-charged system with 5 lbs or less of refrigerant.

PTAC: Packaged Terminal Air Conditioner. Self-contained unit installed through a wall.

Here's why this matters on the job: Many entry-level jobs involve servicing small appliances in hotels, restaurants, and residential settings.

Watch out for these: 5 lbs is the cutoff for small appliances. And — Factory-charged means sealed at the factory

What is the maximum charge that defines a small appliance under EPA 608? That number is on the exam.

Type I covers small appliances. Don't underestimate this section — it has tricky questions.

Type I recovery requirements: 90 percent recovery for operating systems, 80 percent for non-operating systems. Both self-contained and system-dependent recovery equipment can be used. System-dependent recovery uses the appliance own compressor or internal pressure to push refrigerant into the recovery vessel. If the compressor does not work you cannot achieve 90 percent which is why the non-operating requirement is lower at 80 percent.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Self-Contained Recovery: Uses its own compressor to pull refrigerant out of the system.

System-Dependent Recovery: Uses the appliance own compressor or pressure to push refrigerant out.

Here's why this matters on the job: Proper recovery technique prevents EPA violations and environmental damage.

Watch out for these: 90 percent for operating, 80 percent for non-operating. And — System-dependent relies on the appliance compressor working

What is the maximum charge that defines a small appliance under EPA 608? That number is on the exam.

Type I covers small appliances. Don't underestimate this section — it has tricky questions.

Self-contained recovery equipment has its own compressor and can recover refrigerant regardless of whether the appliance compressor works. It connects to the system through access valves or process tubes. The recovered refrigerant goes into an approved DOT recovery cylinder. Self-contained equipment must be certified by an EPA-approved testing organization.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Process Tube: A sealed copper tube on a small appliance that can be pierced for access to the refrigerant circuit.

Access Valve: A valve installed on the system to allow gauge and recovery equipment connection.

Here's why this matters on the job: Self-contained recovery is the more reliable method since it does not depend on the appliance working.

Watch out for these: Equipment must be EPA-certified. And — Recovery cylinders must be DOT-approved

What is the maximum charge that defines a small appliance under EPA 608? That number is on the exam.

Type I covers small appliances. Don't underestimate this section — it has tricky questions.

Small appliances are exempt from the mandatory leak repair requirements that apply to larger systems. However refrigerant must still be recovered before disposal. This exemption exists because the cost of leak repair on a small appliance often exceeds the cost of replacement. Scrap dealers and recyclers must also recover refrigerant before processing appliances.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Leak Repair Exemption: Small appliances are exempt from mandatory leak repair rules but recovery is still required before disposal.

Here's why this matters on the job: Even though leak repair is not required you must still recover refrigerant before disposing of any appliance.

Watch out for these: Exemption is from leak REPAIR not from recovery. And — Scrap dealers must also recover refrigerant

What is the maximum charge that defines a small appliance under EPA 608? That number is on the exam.

Watch out for these: Know the 5 lb cutoff. And — Know 90/80 percent recovery requirements

Type II is the bread and butter of residential HVAC. This is what you'll use every day.

Type II covers high-pressure systems — the most common HVAC equipment including residential AC, commercial rooftop units, and heat pumps. These systems use refrigerants like R-410A, R-22, and R-134a that operate above atmospheric pressure on both the high and low sides. High-pressure systems have service ports for gauge connection and are designed for field service including charging, recovery, and component replacement.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

High-Pressure System: System operating above atmospheric pressure on both sides. Includes most residential and commercial AC.

Here's why this matters on the job: Type II is the most important certification for residential and commercial HVAC technicians.

Watch out for these: Most of your career will involve Type II systems. And — R-410A operates at significantly higher pressures than R-22

What recovery level do you need on a system under 200 pounds using post-1993 equipment? Know that cold.

Type II is the bread and butter of residential HVAC. This is what you'll use every day.

Type II recovery requirements depend on system charge size. Systems under 200 lbs: recover to 0 psig. Systems 200 lbs or more: recover to 10 inches Hg vacuum. These levels apply when using recovery equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993. Recovery must be completed before opening the system for repair. The only exception is de minimis releases during normal connection and disconnection of gauges and hoses.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

De Minimis Release: The small unavoidable release when connecting or disconnecting hoses. This is the only legal release.

Here's why this matters on the job: Know these recovery levels cold for the EPA 608 exam and for every service call.

Watch out for these: Under 200 lbs = 0 psig. And — Over 200 lbs = 10 inches Hg vacuum

What recovery level do you need on a system under 200 pounds using post-1993 equipment? Know that cold.

Type II is the bread and butter of residential HVAC. This is what you'll use every day.

Leak detection methods: electronic leak detectors sense refrigerant vapor and alarm when concentration exceeds the threshold. UV dye is injected into the system and glows under UV light at the leak location. Soap bubbles applied to suspected leak points will bubble when refrigerant escapes. Nitrogen pressure testing pressurizes the system with dry nitrogen to check for pressure drop over time. Standing pressure test leaves the system pressurized overnight and checks for pressure drop the next day.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Electronic Leak Detector: Senses refrigerant vapor concentration. Must be calibrated before each use.

Standing Pressure Test: System pressurized with nitrogen overnight. Pressure drop indicates a leak.

Here's why this matters on the job: Finding leaks is one of the most time-consuming parts of HVAC service. Mastering multiple detection methods makes you more efficient.

Watch out for these: Calibrate electronic detectors before each use. And — Never pressurize with oxygen — explosion risk

What recovery level do you need on a system under 200 pounds using post-1993 equipment? Know that cold.

Type II is the bread and butter of residential HVAC. This is what you'll use every day.

Evacuation removes moisture and non-condensable gases from the system after repair. Connect the vacuum pump through the manifold gauges. Pull vacuum to 500 microns or below. Close the valve and monitor with a micron gauge. The system should hold below 1000 microns for 10 minutes. If it rises above 1000 microns there is either a leak or residual moisture. Triple evacuation (vacuum, break with nitrogen, vacuum, break with nitrogen, final vacuum) is more effective at removing moisture than a single deep vacuum.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Evacuation: Using a vacuum pump to remove moisture and non-condensable gases from the refrigerant circuit.

Triple Evacuation: Pull vacuum, break with nitrogen, repeat three times. More effective at removing moisture.

Micron Gauge: Measures deep vacuum levels. System must hold below 500 microns.

Here's why this matters on the job: Proper evacuation prevents acid formation that destroys compressors. Cutting corners here causes callbacks.

Watch out for these: 500 microns or below is the target. And — Monitor for 10 minutes after closing the valve

What recovery level do you need on a system under 200 pounds using post-1993 equipment? Know that cold.

Type II is the bread and butter of residential HVAC. This is what you'll use every day.

Leak repair requirements for high-pressure comfort cooling systems: if the annual leak rate exceeds 10 percent the owner must repair the leak within 30 days. For commercial refrigeration the trigger is 20 percent. Leak rate is calculated as the amount of refrigerant added divided by the total system charge. If repair is not economically feasible the owner can develop a retrofit or retirement plan.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Leak Rate: Annual refrigerant loss as a percentage of total charge. Comfort cooling trigger is 10 percent.

Here's why this matters on the job: Document all refrigerant added to a system. This data is needed to calculate leak rates and comply with regulations.

Watch out for these: 10 percent for comfort cooling. And — 20 percent for commercial refrigeration

What recovery level do you need on a system under 200 pounds using post-1993 equipment? Know that cold.

Here's why this matters on the job: You will use recovery equipment on every major repair. Speed and proper technique matter.

Watch out for these: Never overfill recovery cylinders past 80 percent. And — Verify recovery is complete before opening the system

Type III covers low-pressure chillers. These are commercial systems — big equipment, big consequences.

Type III covers low-pressure systems primarily centrifugal chillers used in large commercial buildings. These systems use refrigerants like R-11, R-123, and R-245fa that boil at temperatures above typical conditions at atmospheric pressure. Because of this the systems operate below atmospheric pressure (in a vacuum) during normal operation. This means air leaks INTO the system rather than refrigerant leaking out.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Low-Pressure System: Operates below atmospheric pressure. Air leaks in rather than refrigerant leaking out.

Centrifugal Chiller: Large commercial cooling system using a centrifugal compressor and low-pressure refrigerant.

Here's why this matters on the job: Type III is less common for residential technicians but required for Universal certification and commercial building work.

Watch out for these: Low-pressure systems operate in a vacuum. And — Air leaks IN not refrigerant leaks OUT

Why does a low-pressure system have air leaking in rather than refrigerant leaking out? Think about the pressure relationship.

Type III covers low-pressure chillers. These are commercial systems — big equipment, big consequences.

Type III recovery requirements: systems under 200 lbs recover to 0 psig. Systems 200 lbs or more recover to 25 mm Hg absolute. These levels are different from Type II because low-pressure systems operate differently. Recovery from low-pressure systems is slower because the refrigerant is at or below atmospheric pressure and must be heated or pressurized to move it.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

25 mm Hg Absolute: Recovery target for low-pressure systems 200 lbs or more. Measured in absolute pressure.

Here's why this matters on the job: Low-pressure recovery requires patience and proper technique.

Watch out for these: Under 200 lbs = 0 psig. And — Over 200 lbs = 25 mm Hg absolute

Why does a low-pressure system have air leaking in rather than refrigerant leaking out? Think about the pressure relationship.

Type III covers low-pressure chillers. These are commercial systems — big equipment, big consequences.

A purge unit removes air and non-condensable gases that leak into low-pressure systems. Since the system operates below atmospheric pressure air constantly tries to enter through any imperfection in seals, gaskets, or joints. The purge unit monitors the condenser and removes accumulated air. High purge unit run time indicates excessive air leaks that need to be found and repaired.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Purge Unit: Removes air and non-condensables that leak into low-pressure systems.

Here's why this matters on the job: Monitoring purge unit run time is a key maintenance indicator for chiller health.

Watch out for these: High purge run time means excessive air leaks. And — Air in the system raises head pressure and reduces efficiency

Why does a low-pressure system have air leaking in rather than refrigerant leaking out? Think about the pressure relationship.

Type III covers low-pressure chillers. These are commercial systems — big equipment, big consequences.

Water is the enemy of low-pressure systems. Many chillers have water-cooled condensers and water-chilled evaporators with water flowing through tubes inside the refrigerant vessel. A tube leak allows water into the refrigerant circuit causing hydrolysis — water reacting with refrigerant to form acid. This acid destroys motor windings, bearings, and seals. Detecting water contamination early through oil analysis prevents catastrophic damage.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Hydrolysis: Water reacting with refrigerant to form hydrochloric or hydrofluoric acid.

Here's why this matters on the job: Regular oil analysis on chillers catches water contamination before it destroys the compressor.

Watch out for these: Water in the system forms acid. And — Oil analysis detects contamination early

Why does a low-pressure system have air leaking in rather than refrigerant leaking out? Think about the pressure relationship.

Type III covers low-pressure chillers. These are commercial systems — big equipment, big consequences.

Rupture discs are safety devices on low-pressure vessels that burst to relieve pressure if it exceeds safe limits. They prevent catastrophic vessel failure. Once a rupture disc bursts it must be replaced. Never plug or bypass a ruptured disc. The biggest risk when evacuating a chiller is freezing the water in the tubes if water has not been drained. Ice expansion can rupture the tubes causing an extremely expensive repair.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Rupture Disc: Safety device that bursts to relieve dangerous pressure. Must be replaced after activation.

Here's why this matters on the job: Always drain water from chiller tubes before deep evacuation to prevent freeze damage.

Watch out for these: Never bypass a ruptured disc. And — Drain water before evacuating

Three charging methods: Subcooling method for TXV systems — measure liquid line pressure, convert to saturation temp, measure actual liquid line temp, subcooling equals saturation minus actual. Target is typically 10-15F per manufacturer spec. Superheat method for piston systems — use the manufacturer charging chart based on outdoor temp and indoor wet bulb. Weigh-in method — evacuate completely then add exact nameplate charge by weight using a refrigerant scale. Each method has specific conditions where it is most accurate.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Subcooling Method: Charging method for TXV systems. Target typically 10-15F.

Weigh-In Method: Add exact nameplate charge by weight after complete evacuation.

Here's why this matters on the job: Using the wrong charging method is one of the most common technician errors. It leads to overcharge or undercharge and callbacks.

Watch out for these: TXV = subcooling method. And — Piston = superheat method with charging chart

System diagnostics using gauge readings: both pressures high = overcharge or non-condensables. Both pressures low = undercharge. Low suction high discharge = airflow restriction. High suction low discharge = compressor weakness. Normal suction high discharge = dirty condenser. Low suction normal discharge = restricted metering device. Always verify airflow before interpreting pressures because airflow problems mimic charge problems.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Non-Condensables: Air or nitrogen in the system. Takes up condenser space raising head pressure.

Here's why this matters on the job: Pattern recognition with gauge readings is the core diagnostic skill. The faster you can read the pattern the faster you solve the problem.

Watch out for these: Always verify airflow FIRST. And — Both pressures tell a story together

Ductwork design determines system performance. Supply ducts carry conditioned air to rooms. Return ducts bring air back. Total external static pressure (TESP) measures the resistance the blower must overcome. Maximum TESP for most residential systems is 0.50 inches water column. Undersized ducts cause high static pressure, noise, reduced airflow, and comfort complaints. Oversized ducts waste space and money. Duct leaks in unconditioned spaces (attics, crawlspaces) waste 20-30 percent of conditioned air.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

TESP: Total External Static Pressure. Sum of supply and return static. Maximum 0.50 in w.c. for most residential.

CFM: Cubic Feet per Minute. Airflow volume. Typical residential is 400 CFM per ton of cooling.

Here's why this matters on the job: Many comfort complaints are duct problems not equipment problems. Measuring static pressure identifies duct issues.

Watch out for these: 0.50 in w.c. maximum TESP. And — 400 CFM per ton is the standard airflow target

Manual J calculates the heating and cooling load of a building based on insulation, windows, orientation, climate, and occupancy. The result determines the correct equipment size in tons (1 ton = 12,000 BTU/hr). Oversized equipment short-cycles causing poor humidity control and uneven temperatures. Undersized equipment runs continuously and cannot maintain comfort on extreme days. Manual D sizes the ductwork based on the Manual J load calculation. Manual S selects the specific equipment model.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Manual J: ACCA load calculation determining required heating and cooling capacity.

Ton: Unit of cooling capacity. 1 ton = 12,000 BTU per hour.

Short Cycling: System turning on and off too frequently. Caused by oversized equipment.

Here's why this matters on the job: Understanding load calculations helps you identify improperly sized systems — a common cause of comfort complaints.

Watch out for these: Bigger is NOT better — oversized systems cause humidity problems. And — 1 ton = 12,000 BTU/hr

Brazing joins copper tubing using a filler metal that melts above 840F. Silver-bearing brazing alloys (such as Silfos) are used for copper-to-copper joints. Always flow dry nitrogen through the tubing during brazing to prevent copper oxide formation inside. Copper oxide flakes contaminate the system and clog metering devices. Soldering uses lower temperatures (below 840F) and is used for water piping not refrigerant lines. Proper joint preparation: cut square, deburr inside and outside, clean with emery cloth, apply flux if required, heat the joint evenly, and let the filler metal flow by capillary action.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Brazing: Joining copper tubing with filler metal above 840F. Used for refrigerant lines.

Nitrogen Purge: Flowing dry nitrogen through tubing during brazing to prevent copper oxide formation.

Here's why this matters on the job: A bad braze joint leaks refrigerant. A joint brazed without nitrogen purge contaminates the system. Both cause callbacks.

Watch out for these: ALWAYS purge with nitrogen during brazing. And — Heat the tube not the filler metal

Line set installation connects the outdoor and indoor units with copper refrigerant lines. The liquid line (smaller) carries warm high-pressure liquid. The suction line (larger) carries cold low-pressure vapor and must be insulated. Proper installation: measure and cut to length, deburr, make flare connections or braze joints, pressure test with nitrogen, evacuate to 500 microns, and charge. Line sets must be supported every 6-8 feet and protected from physical damage.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Line Set: Pre-insulated copper tubing pair connecting outdoor and indoor units.

Flare Connection: Mechanical joint using a flared tube end and flare nut. No heat required.

Here's why this matters on the job: Clean line set installation prevents leaks and contamination. Sloppy work causes callbacks.

Watch out for these: Insulate the suction line completely. And — Support lines every 6-8 feet

System commissioning verifies everything works correctly after installation. Checklist: verify refrigerant charge using superheat or subcooling method. Measure total external static pressure (must be under 0.50 in w.c.). Measure temperature split at supply and return (14-22F for cooling). Measure amp draw on compressor and blower (compare to nameplate). Test all safety controls. Verify thermostat operation in all modes. Document everything on the commissioning form.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Commissioning: Systematic verification that all system components are installed and operating correctly.

Here's why this matters on the job: Proper commissioning prevents callbacks. A system that is not commissioned properly will have problems within the first season.

Watch out for these: Check TESP, temperature split, amp draw, and charge. And — Test ALL modes: cooling, heating, fan only

Troubleshooting is a skill, not a guess. We're going to build your diagnostic process from the ground up.

Systematic troubleshooting follows a logical process instead of guessing. Step 1: Verify the complaint — what exactly is the customer experiencing? Step 2: Check the simple things first — thermostat settings, power, filter. Step 3: Gather data — measure temperatures, pressures, amp draw, voltage. Step 4: Analyze the data — compare measurements to normal values. Step 5: Identify the root cause — not just the symptom. Step 6: Repair and verify — fix the problem and confirm the system operates correctly. Step 7: Document — write the service report. Most callbacks happen because technicians skip steps 1, 5, or 7.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Root Cause: The underlying reason for the failure, not just the symptom. A frozen coil is a symptom. Low charge or dirty filter is the root cause.

Here's why this matters on the job: Systematic troubleshooting is what separates a professional technician from a parts changer. Employers value technicians who find root causes.

Watch out for these: Always verify the complaint first. And — Check simple things before connecting gauges

Common AC failures and their diagnostic patterns. No cooling at all: check power, contactor, capacitor, compressor. System runs but does not cool: check charge (superheat/subcooling), airflow (filter, blower), and metering device. Frozen evaporator: low airflow (dirty filter) or low charge (leak). Short cycling: oversized system, low charge tripping low-pressure switch, or thermostat location. High electric bills: dirty condenser, low charge causing long run times, or duct leaks. Water leaks: clogged condensate drain, frozen coil melting, or cracked drain pan.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Short Cycling: System turning on and off rapidly. Causes include oversized equipment, low charge, and thermostat issues.

Here's why this matters on the job: Pattern recognition speeds up diagnosis. After enough service calls you will recognize common failure patterns immediately.

Watch out for these: No cooling = power, contactor, capacitor, compressor. And — Frozen coil = airflow or charge

You get a call — condenser fan not spinning, compressor humming. What's the first thing you check? Answer that before you move on.

Common heating failures. No heat from gas furnace: dirty flame sensor (most common), failed igniter, tripped safety switch, gas valve issue. Read the control board fault codes first. Furnace short cycling: dirty filter tripping limit switch, blocked return air, or failed blower motor. No heat from heat pump: stuck reversing valve, low charge, failed compressor, or defrost board failure. Aux heat running constantly: heat pump problem forcing backup heat. Strange smells: possible cracked heat exchanger leaking combustion gases.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Fault Code: LED flash pattern on the furnace control board indicating the specific failure. Always read codes first.

Here's why this matters on the job: Winter no-heat calls are urgent. Customers are cold and stressed. Fast accurate diagnosis builds your reputation.

Watch out for these: Read fault codes FIRST on every furnace call. And — Dirty flame sensor is the number one no-heat cause

OSHA 10-Hour Construction Safety is delivered through CareerSafe, an authorized OSHA Education Center. You complete the coursework online through CareerSafe's platform and receive a DOL wallet card upon passing. The course covers the major hazards HVAC technicians face on job sites. The four leading causes of construction fatalities (the Fatal Four) are falls, struck-by objects, electrocution, and caught-in/between. Together these account for over 60 percent of construction deaths. Fall protection is required at 6 feet in construction. Proper ladder use, scaffolding safety, and personal fall arrest systems prevent fall injuries.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Fatal Four: Falls, struck-by, electrocution, caught-in/between. Over 60 percent of construction deaths.

Fall Protection: Required at 6 feet in construction. Includes guardrails, safety nets, and personal fall arrest systems.

Here's why this matters on the job: OSHA 10 is required by most HVAC employers. The DOL wallet card proves you completed the training.

Watch out for these: 6 feet is the fall protection trigger in construction. And — Know the Fatal Four

Electrical safety for HVAC technicians: lockout/tagout procedures, arc flash awareness, proper use of insulated tools, and working near energized equipment. OSHA requires de-energization and LOTO before working on electrical systems. If energized work is necessary (such as taking measurements) proper PPE including arc-rated clothing and face shields is required. Ground fault circuit interrupters (GFCIs) are required for all temporary power on construction sites.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Arc Flash: Electrical explosion through air causing severe burns. Proper PPE and de-energization prevent it.

GFCI: Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter. Trips in milliseconds if current leaks to ground. Required on construction sites.

Here's why this matters on the job: Electrical accidents are the third leading cause of construction death. LOTO saves lives.

Watch out for these: Always LOTO before electrical work. And — Verify zero energy with YOUR meter

Hazard communication (HazCom) and the Globally Harmonized System (GHS). Safety Data Sheets (SDS) have 16 standardized sections covering chemical identification, hazards, first aid, fire-fighting, handling, exposure controls, and disposal. GHS pictograms identify hazard types at a glance. HVAC technicians work with refrigerants, brazing fluxes, solvents, and cleaning chemicals that all have SDS requirements. Employers must maintain SDS for every chemical on site and train workers on their contents.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

SDS: Safety Data Sheet. 16 sections covering chemical hazards, handling, and emergency procedures.

GHS Pictogram: Standardized symbols identifying chemical hazard types.

Here's why this matters on the job: You must know how to find and read an SDS for any chemical you work with.

Watch out for these: 16 sections in every SDS. And — Know where SDS are kept on your job site

Confined space entry, excavation safety, scaffolding, and ladder safety. Permit-required confined spaces have limited entry/exit and may contain hazardous atmospheres. HVAC technicians encounter confined spaces in mechanical rooms, boiler rooms, and some crawl spaces. Atmospheric testing is required before entry. Excavation safety applies when installing underground ductwork or piping. Scaffolding must be erected by competent persons and inspected before each use.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Confined Space: Space with limited entry/exit that may contain hazardous atmospheres. Requires permit and atmospheric testing.

Competent Person: OSHA-defined person capable of identifying hazards and authorized to take corrective action.

Here's why this matters on the job: Confined space incidents are often fatal. Never enter without proper procedures and atmospheric testing.

Watch out for these: Test atmosphere before entering any confined space. And — Never enter alone

Fire prevention and hot work permits. Brazing and soldering create fire hazards. Hot work permits are required when working near combustible materials. A fire watch must be maintained for 30 minutes after hot work is completed. Fire extinguisher types: Class A for ordinary combustibles, Class B for flammable liquids, Class C for electrical fires, Class D for combustible metals. Know the PASS technique: Pull pin, Aim at base, Squeeze handle, Sweep side to side.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Hot Work Permit: Required authorization for brazing, welding, or soldering near combustible materials.

PASS: Fire extinguisher technique: Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep.

Here's why this matters on the job: HVAC technicians braze copper tubing regularly. Fire prevention is part of every brazing job.

Watch out for these: Fire watch for 30 minutes after hot work. And — Know which extinguisher for which fire type

Personal protective equipment selection and use. Safety glasses for all HVAC work. Gloves appropriate to the task: leather for handling sheet metal, heat-resistant for brazing, chemical-resistant for refrigerant handling. Steel-toe boots on all job sites. Hearing protection near compressors and power tools. Respiratory protection when working with chemicals or in dusty environments. Hard hats on construction sites.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

PPE: Personal Protective Equipment selected based on the specific hazards of each task.

Here's why this matters on the job: PPE is not optional. Employers provide it but you must wear it. Violations result in discipline or termination.

Watch out for these: Match PPE to the specific hazard. And — Safety glasses are required for ALL HVAC work

Rigging and material handling for HVAC equipment. Rooftop units, condensers, and air handlers are heavy. Proper lifting techniques prevent back injuries. Rigging: Using ropes, chains, slings, and cranes to lift and move heavy equipment safely.

Here's why this matters on the job: HVAC equipment is heavy. Improper lifting causes career-ending back injuries.

Watch out for these: Know the weight of equipment before lifting. And — Use mechanical assistance for anything over 50 lbs

Adult CPR: check for responsiveness, call 911, check for breathing, begin compressions. 30 compressions at 100-120 per minute, 2 inches deep, allowing full chest recoil. 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions. Continue until AED arrives or EMS takes over. AED use: power on, attach pads, stand clear during analysis, deliver shock if advised, immediately resume CPR.

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

CPR: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation. 30 compressions to 2 breaths at 100-120 per minute.

AED: Automated External Defibrillator. Analyzes heart rhythm and delivers shock if needed.

Here's why this matters on the job: HVAC technicians work in extreme heat, at heights, and near electricity. Cardiac events, heat stroke, and electrical shock are real risks.

Watch out for these: 100-120 compressions per minute. And — 2 inches deep with full recoil

First aid for HVAC-specific injuries: electrical shock (disconnect power first, never touch the victim while energized), burns from brazing and hot surfaces (cool with water, cover with sterile dressing), refrigerant frostbite (warm gradually, do not rub), heat exhaustion and heat stroke (move to shade, cool rapidly, call 911 for heat stroke), cuts from sheet metal (direct pressure, clean wound), and eye injuries from debris or chemicals (flush with water for 15 minutes).

Here are the key terms. Write these down.

Heat Stroke: Life-threatening emergency. Hot red dry skin, confusion. Call 911 and cool immediately.

Heat Exhaustion: Heavy sweating, weakness, nausea. Move to shade, hydrate, rest.

Here's why this matters on the job: Knowing first aid for trade-specific injuries can save your life or a coworkers life.

Watch out for these: Disconnect power BEFORE touching an electrical shock victim. And — Heat stroke is a 911 emergency
